behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is the real filesystem watcher’s recursive behavior across directories. In-process fakes can miss OS-specific watcher semantics (recursive support, path emission differences). MOCK-HIDE applies because mocked event streams can trivially emit subdirectory paths and hide real watcher misconfiguration/failure.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temporary watched root on the host OS, create a nested subdirectory, write a file into that subdirectory, and assert the transformed output appears in the output directory exactly once.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily module handoff/state behavior (request intake + dedup store + time window), not inherently OS-bound. A deterministic in-process test with fake clock can reproduce real failure (e.g., wrong keying, window boundary handling). INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no real OS/network/subprocess required by behavior text, (b) no AI/ML output dependency, (c) no multi-agentic chain, (d) no LLM-governed handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the request-ingest path and dedup component with a controllable clock; submit two requests with identical canonical file path inside the dedup window and assert only one pipeline dispatch occurs, then advance clock beyond window and assert a new dispatch is allowed.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System because behavior text is about "deduplication logic" and "processing request" timing, not real filesystem event semantics.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on OS event emission patterns. Real duplicate/chattery event behavior is exactly what mocks hide, so minimum boundary is real watcher + real filesystem writes.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real watched directory; perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple FS events (e.g., temp-write+rename or chunked write) and assert exactly one processing/output record for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is OS-level watcher lifecycle behavior (inode/handle invalidation and rebind after delete/recreate). In-process fakes generally cannot reproduce true watcher detach/re-attach edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running against a real watched path, delete the directory, recreate it at the same path, then place a new file and assert it is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: The ordering contract is tied to filesystem timestamp behavior under concurrent arrival; real timestamp granularity and event timing can differ by OS/filesystem and can break ordering despite passing mocked tests.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files near-simultaneously in a real watched directory with controlled creation times (or staggered creation with known ordering), then assert processing order matches observed creation timestamps used by the daemon.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected Integration because behavior text anchors ordering to "creation timestamps" for simultaneously arriving real files, which can fail due to real FS timestamp/event characteristics.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This requires real write-in-progress behavior and actual filesystem event timing/locking semantics. Mocked writes/events often make the file appear atomically complete and hide partial-read races.
behaviors.test_description: Begin a slow/chunked write to a file in the real watched directory, ensure arrival event occurs before final close, and assert daemon delays processing until write completion (no partial transform output before close).
